schools.summary: The age and life of Sarah: 'and Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old: these were the years of the life of Sarah' — Sarah's full age a hundred and twenty-seven years (she the only woman in Scripture whose age at death is recorded, the honor given to the matriarch of the covenant, the years of her life summed up); the special recording of Sarah's age and life ('these were the years of the life of Sarah') marking the honor due to her — the wife of Abraham, the mother of the promised seed, the matriarch of Israel (Isaiah 51:2, 'look unto Abraham your father, and unto Sarah that bare you,' the dignity of Sarah, commemorated, the mother of the faithful, 1 Peter 3:6); Sarah's long life (127 years), thirty-seven years after the birth of Isaac (he being now thirty-seven, the honored matriarch, the recording of her years a token of her place in the covenant history, the close of Sarah's life, the first death in the patriarchal family recorded) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Sarah was an hundred and seven and twenty years old: these were the years of the life of Sarah
the age and life of Sarah
the only woman whose age at death is recorded, the honor to the matriarch
the mother of the promised seed and of the faithful (Isaiah 51:2; 1 Peter 3:6)

schools.summary: Sarah dies, Abraham mourns: 'and Sarah died in Kirjatharba; the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan: and Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her' — Sarah dying at Hebron (Kirjath-arba, 'the city of Arba,' a notable Hittite/Anakite town, the death of the matriarch in the land of promise, where Abraham sojourned, the first death recorded in the patriarchal family); 'Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her' (the patriarch mourning and weeping for his wife, the genuine grief of Abraham for his beloved companion of so many years, the lawful, natural sorrow of the godly at the death of loved ones, not the hopeless grief of the world, 1 Thessalonians 4:13, but real grief, the affection of Abraham for Sarah); Abraham's mourning and weeping showing that the godly may and do grieve at the death of the dear (the natural affection sanctified by faith, the patriarch's sorrow, yet, as the chapter shows, joined with the hope of the resurrection, burying his dead in the land of promise, the example of godly grief, tempered by hope) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Sarah died in Kirjatharba; the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan: and Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her
Sarah's death and Abraham's mourning
the death of the matriarch in the land of promise
the godly grief tempered by hope (1 Thessalonians 4:13)

schools.summary: Abraham rises to seek a burying-place: 'and Abraham stood up from before his dead, and spake unto the sons of Heth, saying' — Abraham rising from beside the body of Sarah (his 'dead') and going to speak to the sons of Heth (the Hittites, the inhabitants of Hebron) about a burying-place (the patriarch, in his grief, attending to the burial, the dealing with the people of the land for a tomb); Abraham 'stood up from before his dead' (turning from his mourning to the duty of burial, the care of the godly for the decent burial of the dead, a token of respect for the body and of the hope of the resurrection, the patriarch rising from grief to action); 'spake unto the sons of Heth' (Abraham addressing the Hittites, the people of the land, the children of Heth, 10:15, to obtain a burying-place, the courteous, careful dealing with the inhabitants, the seeking of a possession, a tomb, in the land of promise, the negotiation for the cave of Machpelah, vv.4-16) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, JFB.

themes: And Abraham stood up from before his dead, and spake unto the sons of Heth, saying
Abraham rising to seek a burying-place from the sons of Heth
the care of the godly for the decent burial of the dead
turning from grief to the duty of burial, dealing with the people of the land

schools.summary: Abraham a stranger and pilgrim, seeking a possession: 'I am a stranger and a sojourner with you: give me a possession of a buryingplace with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight' — Abraham confessing himself 'a stranger and a sojourner with you' (a resident alien in the land, though it was the land of promise, to be his seed's inheritance, he possessing none of it, Acts 7:5, and dwelling as a pilgrim, the confession of faith — Abraham and the patriarchs 'confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth,' seeking 'a better country, that is, an heavenly,' Hebrews 11:13-16, the pilgrim character of the man of faith); Abraham seeking 'a possession of a buryingplace' (to buy a tomb, his own possession, in the land, notably, the only piece of the promised land Abraham would possess being a grave, the cave of Machpelah, vv.9, 17-20, yet the buying of a burying-place in the land an act of faith — a pledge and earnest of the future inheritance, and a confession of the hope of the resurrection, laying his dead in the land God had promised to his seed, in expectation of rising in it, the stranger seeking a possession in the land of promise); 'that I may bury my dead out of my sight' (Abraham seeking to bury Sarah decently, the care for the body, the seeking of a tomb in the land, the burial in faith and hope) — Keil and Delitzsch, Clarke, Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What is the significance of Abraham the stranger seeking a burying-place?

sides.position: Abraham a stranger and pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13), possessing none of the land (Acts 7:5), yet buying a burying-place - his only possession in the land of promise a grave, the purchase an act of faith, a pledge of the future inheritance and the hope of the resurrection

themes: I am a stranger and a sojourner with you: give me a possession of a buryingplace with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight
Abraham's confession as a stranger and pilgrim, seeking a burying-place (the crux)
Abraham a pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13), possessing none of the land (Acts 7:5), yet buying a grave
the purchase an act of faith, a pledge of the inheritance and the hope of the resurrection

schools.summary: The Hittites answer Abraham: 'and the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him' — the Hittites (the people of the land, v.3) responding to Abraham's request (v.4) with respect and courtesy (the introduction to their honorable answer, v.6, the people of the land dealing kindly with the patriarch); the Hittites answering Abraham courteously and respectfully (the favor and esteem in which the patriarch was held among them, a mighty prince, v.6, the honorable dealing of the people of the land with the stranger); the children of Heth's answer (vv.5-6) showing the respect and goodwill the Hittites bore Abraham (the providence giving the patriarch favor among the people of the land, as with Abimelech, 21:22, the courteous negotiation for the burying-place) — Gill.

themes: And the children of Heth answered Abraham, saying unto him
the Hittites' courteous answer to Abraham
the favor and esteem in which the patriarch was held
the providence giving Abraham favor among the people of the land

schools.summary: The Hittites honor Abraham as a mighty prince: 'hear us, my lord: thou art a mighty prince among us: in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead; none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre, but that thou mayest bury thy dead' — the Hittites honoring Abraham as 'a mighty prince among us (a prince of God)' (literally, 'a prince of God,' a great and honored man, blessed of God, the esteem in which the patriarch was held among the people of the land, the providence giving Abraham favor and dignity, as Abimelech had owned, 21:22, the recognition of God's blessing on him); the Hittites offering Abraham the choice of their sepulchres — 'in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead' (the generous offer of any tomb he wished, the honor and goodwill of the people, the free offer of a burying-place); 'none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre' (none refusing Abraham a tomb, the unanimous goodwill of the Hittites, the honor done to the patriarch, the favor that opened the way for the purchase of Machpelah, vv.7-16) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Hear us, my lord: thou art a mighty prince among us: in the choice of our sepulchres bury thy dead; none of us shall withhold from thee his sepulchre
the Hittites honoring Abraham as a mighty prince
Abraham honored as 'a prince of God,' blessed of God (21:22)
the generous offer and unanimous goodwill of the people of the land

schools.summary: Abraham bows to the people of the land: 'and Abraham stood up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth' — Abraham, in response to the Hittites' honorable offer (v.6), rising and bowing to them in courteous acknowledgment (the patriarch's politeness and humility, the gracious dealing of Abraham with the people of the land, the courtesy of the godly man); Abraham 'bowed himself to the people of the land' (a token of respect and gratitude for their goodwill, the humility and courtesy of the great patriarch toward the Hittites, the godly man honoring others, Romans 12:10, the gracious manners of Abraham); Abraham's bowing preparing for his renewed request (to buy the cave of Machpelah from Ephron, vv.8-9, the courteous negotiation, the patriarch's humble, gracious dealing in obtaining the burying-place, the example of courtesy and humility) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Abraham stood up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth
Abraham's courteous bowing to the people of the land
the humility and courtesy of the great patriarch
the godly man honoring others (Romans 12:10)

schools.summary: Abraham asks the Hittites to intercede with Ephron: 'and he communed with them, saying, If it be your mind that I should bury my dead out of my sight; hear me, and intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar' — Abraham asking the Hittites, if they were willing he should bury his dead, to intercede for him with Ephron (the owner of the cave he desired, the courteous request through the people of the land, the patriarch's careful, honorable dealing); Abraham asking their goodwill ('if it be your mind') — the courteous seeking of their consent and help (the gracious dealing of the patriarch, the asking of the people's favor); 'intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar' (Abraham asking them to entreat, intercede with, Ephron, the owner of the cave of Machpelah, v.9, to sell it to him, the seeking of the specific tomb through the mediation of the people, the courteous, careful negotiation for the burying-place, the patriarch's particular desire for Machpelah) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he communed with them, saying, If it be your mind that I should bury my dead out of my sight; hear me, and intreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar
Abraham asking the Hittites to intercede with Ephron
the courteous request through the people of the land
seeking to buy the cave of Machpelah from its owner (v.9)

schools.summary: Abraham seeks to buy the cave of Machpelah: 'that he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he hath, which is in the end of his field; for as much money as it is worth he shall give it me for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you' — Abraham naming the specific tomb he desired: the cave of Machpelah (the 'double' cave), belonging to Ephron, at the end of his field (the patriarch's particular request, the cave that would become the burying-place of the patriarchs, the tomb of Sarah, Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, Leah, 49:31; 50:13); Abraham buying the cave 'for as much money as it is worth' (at the full, fair price, he not taking it as a gift, v.11, but paying its value, the honest, scrupulous dealing of the patriarch, that the possession might be securely and rightfully his, against future dispute, the integrity of Abraham in the purchase); 'for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you' (Abraham seeking the cave as a secure possession, a permanent burying-place, his own, the first possession in the land of promise, a grave, vv.17-20, the pledge of the future inheritance, the burying-place of the patriarchs in the land) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: That he may give me the cave of Machpelah... for as much money as it is worth he shall give it me for a possession of a buryingplace amongst you
Abraham seeking to buy the cave of Machpelah at full price
the honest dealing of the patriarch, paying the full value, not taking a gift
the secure possession, the burying-place of the patriarchs (49:31), the first in the land

schools.summary: Ephron answers Abraham publicly: 'and Ephron dwelt among the children of Heth: and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, even of all that went in at the gate of his city, saying' — Ephron (the owner of the cave) present among the Hittites, and answering Abraham publicly, in the hearing of all the people gathered at the city gate (the place of public business and witness, the public, formal negotiation, the transaction witnessed by the whole assembly); the answer given 'in the audience' of the Hittites — publicly, before all (the transaction conducted openly, with many witnesses, the legal validity secured by the public dealing, the gate the place of legal business); 'all that went in at the gate of his city' (the city gate, the place of public concourse, business, and judgment, the negotiation conducted there, before all the people, the public witness securing the purchase, vv.17-18, the formal, witnessed transaction) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Ephron... answered Abraham in the audience of the children of Heth, even of all that went in at the gate of his city
Ephron answering Abraham publicly
the public, formal negotiation at the city gate, before all the people
the transaction witnessed, securing the purchase (vv.17-18)

schools.summary: Ephron offers the field and cave: 'nay, my lord, hear me: the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee; in the presence of the sons of my people give I it thee: bury thy dead' — Ephron offering to give Abraham not only the cave but the whole field (a more generous offer, or an Eastern courtesy of seeming to give while expecting payment, the public profession of generosity, the offer of the field and cave as a gift); Ephron making the offer 'in the presence of the sons of my people' — publicly, before witnesses (whether the offer was genuine generosity, or, as the Eastern manner, a polite form expecting payment, the naming of the price, v.15, suggesting the latter, the public dealing); 'bury thy dead' (Ephron bidding Abraham bury Sarah, the courteous granting of the burying-place, whether as a gift or a sale, the cave to be Abraham's, the courteous offer that Abraham, in his integrity, would not accept as a gift but would pay for, vv.13-16) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Nay, my lord, hear me: the field give I thee, and the cave that is therein, I give it thee... bury thy dead
Ephron's offer to give Abraham the field and cave
the public offer (whether genuine generosity or Eastern courtesy expecting payment)
the courteous granting, which Abraham would insist on buying (vv.13-16)

schools.summary: Abraham bows again to the people: 'and Abraham bowed down himself before the people of the land' — Abraham again bowing (as v.7) in courteous acknowledgment of the Hittites' (and Ephron's) goodwill (the patriarch's politeness and humility, the gracious dealing of Abraham, the renewed courtesy before he insisted on paying, v.13); Abraham bowing in respect and gratitude (the humility of the great patriarch toward the people of the land, the courteous manners of the godly man, Romans 12:10); 'before the people of the land' (Abraham's courteous bowing before the Hittites preparing for his renewed, firm request to buy the cave, v.13, the patriarch's gracious yet resolute dealing, the courtesy joined with integrity, insisting on paying) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And Abraham bowed down himself before the people of the land
Abraham again bowing courteously to the people
the humility and gracious manners of the patriarch (Romans 12:10)
the courtesy joined with integrity, before insisting on paying (v.13)

schools.summary: Abraham insists on paying for the field: 'and he spake unto Ephron in the audience of the people of the land, saying, But if thou wilt give it, I pray thee, hear me: I will give thee money for the field; take it of me, and I will bury my dead there' — Abraham insisting on paying for the field (he not accepting it as a gift, v.11, but buying it at full price, the patriarch's integrity, that the possession might be securely and rightfully his, the firm, honest dealing); Abraham saying 'take it of me' (pressing Ephron to accept payment, the scrupulous integrity of the patriarch, that he might owe nothing and hold the possession by right of purchase, against future dispute, as the seizing of his well, 21:25, the honest, careful dealing, the refusal to be enriched by another, cf. 14:23); 'I will bury my dead there' (Abraham burying Sarah in the field he bought, the secure burying-place, the possession rightfully purchased, the integrity that secured the tomb of the patriarchs, vv.17-20, by honest payment) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: But if thou wilt give it, I pray thee, hear me: I will give thee money for the field; take it of me, and I will bury my dead there
Abraham insisting on paying for the field
the scrupulous integrity, holding the possession by right of purchase (14:23; 21:25)
the secure burying-place, rightfully purchased

schools.summary: Ephron answers, about to name the price: 'and Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him' — Ephron responding to Abraham's insistence on paying (v.13, the introduction to his naming of the price, v.15, the seller now stating the value of the field, the transaction proceeding to the price); Ephron, having offered the field as a 'gift' (v.11), now (when Abraham insisted on paying) naming its price (v.15, the Eastern manner of bargaining — the seeming gift, then the price, the seller's response, revealing the real expectation of payment); Ephron's answer (vv.14-15) naming the value of the field (four hundred shekels, the seller stating the price, in the public dealing, the transaction proceeding to the payment and securing of the possession, v.16) — Gill, Henry.

themes: And Ephron answered Abraham, saying unto him
Ephron's naming of the price
the turn from the seeming gift to the price (the Eastern manner of bargaining)
the transaction proceeding to the payment and securing of the possession

schools.summary: Ephron names the price: 'my lord, hearken unto me: the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver; what is that betwixt me and thee? bury therefore thy dead' — Ephron naming the price: four hundred shekels of silver (a considerable sum, the value of the field stated, the seller, having professed to give it, v.11, now naming a full, perhaps high, price, the transaction at the stated value); Ephron adding 'what is that betwixt me and thee?' (a polite, dismissive phrase, as if the sum were trifling between friends, the Eastern courtesy of seeming to make light of the price while naming it, the manner of the bargaining, the seller's professed indifference to the substantial sum); 'bury therefore thy dead' (Ephron bidding Abraham bury Sarah, the field being his at the price, the granting of the burying-place upon the agreed value, the transaction concluded, awaiting Abraham's payment, v.16) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: My lord, hearken unto me: the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver; what is that betwixt me and thee? bury therefore thy dead
Ephron naming the price of four hundred shekels
the Eastern courtesy making light of the substantial sum
the field to be Abraham's upon payment

schools.summary: Abraham weighs out the full price: 'and Abraham hearkened unto Ephron; and Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver, which he had named in the audience of the sons of Heth, four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant' — Abraham accepting the price and weighing out (in those days silver was weighed, not coined) the four hundred shekels (the full payment, without bargaining down, the patriarch's prompt, honest payment of the named price, the securing of the purchase); the payment made 'in the audience of the sons of Heth' — publicly, before witnesses (the legal validity of the purchase secured by the public weighing of the money, the transaction witnessed and binding, vv.17-18, the careful, formal dealing); 'four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant' (the full, named price, in current, standard, accepted money, Abraham paying the whole, in good silver, the honest, complete payment, the possession bought at full value, securely his, vv.17-20) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Abraham hearkened unto Ephron; and Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver... four hundred shekels of silver, current money with the merchant
Abraham weighing out the full price for the field
the full payment without bargaining down, in good current money
the public, witnessed transaction securing the possession (vv.17-20)

schools.summary: The field and cave made sure: 'and the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre, the field, and the cave which was therein, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all the borders round about, were made sure' — the property described in legal detail: the field in Machpelah, near Mamre (Hebron), with the cave and all the trees in its borders (the careful, full description of the purchase, the legal conveyance of the property); the detailed enumeration (the field, the cave, the trees, the borders) — the full, legal description of the conveyed property (the completeness of the transaction, the careful securing of every part); 'were made sure' (the property legally and securely conveyed, established, confirmed, to Abraham, the formal, witnessed transfer, v.18, the possession made sure and rightfully his, against future dispute, the first secure possession of Abraham in the land of promise, a burying-place) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah... the field, and the cave which was therein, and all the trees... were made sure
the field and cave made sure to Abraham
the full, legal description and conveyance of the property
the first secure possession of the patriarch in the land of promise

schools.summary: The possession secured to Abraham: 'unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate of his city' — the property made sure (v.17) unto Abraham 'for a possession' (his own, secure holding), publicly, in the presence of the Hittites and all who passed the city gate (the legal, witnessed conveyance, the possession formally and securely Abraham's); the field Abraham's 'for a possession' (a secure, permanent holding, the first piece of the promised land the patriarch possessed, a burying-place, vv.4, 9, the pledge of the future inheritance, the possession by right of purchase); the conveyance made publicly, before all the Hittites and all who passed the gate (the legal validity secured by the many witnesses, the transaction binding and unquestionable, the careful, formal securing of the possession, the first possession of Abraham in the land, openly and rightfully his) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Unto Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all that went in at the gate of his city
the possession secured to Abraham before witnesses
the first secure possession of the patriarch in the land of promise
the legal, binding, witnessed conveyance, the pledge of the inheritance

schools.summary: Abraham buries Sarah in Machpelah: 'and after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre: the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan' — Abraham burying Sarah in the cave he had bought (the burial of the matriarch in the land of promise, the cave of Machpelah, at Hebron, now the tomb of Sarah, and later of Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, Leah, 49:31; 50:13, the patriarchal burying-place); the place — the cave of Machpelah, near Mamre (Hebron, the secure burying-place, bought and rightfully Abraham's, the tomb of the patriarchs in the land); 'in the land of Canaan' (Sarah buried in the land God had promised to Abraham's seed, the burial in the land of promise an act of faith and hope, the patriarchs buried in the land, in expectation of the inheritance and the resurrection, the matriarch laid in the promised land, the first burial in the patriarchal tomb) — Gill.

themes: And after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre: the same is Hebron in the land of Canaan
Abraham burying Sarah in the cave of Machpelah
the patriarchal burying-place, the tomb of Sarah and the patriarchs (49:31)
the burial in the land of promise, an act of faith and hope

schools.summary: The field and cave confirmed to Abraham: 'and the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham for a possession of a buryingplace by the sons of Heth' — the closing summary confirming that the field and cave were securely conveyed to Abraham, by the Hittites, as his possession and burying-place (the legal, binding transfer concluded, the possession made sure); the cave Abraham's 'for a possession of a buryingplace' (the secure, permanent burying-place of the patriarch and his house, the first possession in the land of promise, a grave, vv.4, 9, the pledge of the future inheritance, the burying-place of the patriarchs); 'by the sons of Heth' (the conveyance confirmed by the Hittites, the witnesses and the seller, the legal, public securing of the possession, the close of the chapter, Abraham's faith — buying and possessing a burying-place in the land of promise, the pledge of the inheritance and the hope of the resurrection, Hebrews 11:13-16, 19, the first possession of the patriarch in the land) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the field, and the cave that is therein, were made sure unto Abraham for a possession of a buryingplace by the sons of Heth
the field and cave confirmed to Abraham as a burying-place
the first secure possession of the patriarch in the land of promise (a grave)
the pledge of the inheritance and the hope of the resurrection (Hebrews 11:13-16, 19)
